political.s: <cite index="83-17">Eli Lilly contributed $1,863,367 in the 2024 cycle</cite>, demonstrating mixed political engagement. <cite index="81-1">The company spent $11,155,000 lobbying in 2025</cite>, focusing on pharmaceutical-related policy issues. <cite index="89-1,89-5">The company increased lobbying spending to $7 million in 2021 as Democrats considered cracking down on insulin prices</cite>.

charity.s: <cite index="13-7">In 2023 alone, the Lilly Foundation matched over $6.2 million in contributions</cite>. <cite index="15-17">In 2024, the Lilly Foundation matched over $5.1 million in eligible Lilly employee and retiree contributions</cite>. <cite index="20-4">The company provided $4.2 billion in free medicines in 2024 including $30.9 million in disaster relief and humanitarian assistance</cite>.
charity.focus: Health equity, education, STEM, disaster relief, international health

environment.s: <cite index="22-1">Eli Lilly reduced Scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions by 37% from 2020 to 2024, while overall business grew</cite>. <cite index="28-1">In 2024, Lilly repurposed 95.7% of its plastic waste and recycled 86.6%</cite>. <cite index="26-5">By 2030, the company aims for 100% purchased electricity from renewable sources and carbon neutrality in own operations</cite>.

labor.s: <cite index="2-9">As of 2025, the company employed more than 50,000 workers worldwide</cite>. <cite index="33-3,33-4">Lilly supports standards that people should have the right to associate freely and bargain collectively, and maintains a practice of complying with local minimum age laws without child labor or forced labor in any facilities globally</cite>. Limited public OSHA/NLRB data available for specific findings.

dei.s: <cite index="51-3,51-4">At Lilly, the company believes in the power of diversity, equity and inclusion to fulfill its purpose, by leveraging diverse backgrounds of employees and driving measurable strategies to improve DEI, including diversity within clinical trials</cite>. <cite index="59-3">Eli Lilly removed 'DEI' from its annual proxy statement while emphasizing workforce diversity</cite>, indicating recent strategic rebranding amid changing legal environment.

animals.s: <cite index="43-1,43-2,43-3">Eli Lilly has hurt and killed thousands of animals in various tests; in 2008 alone the company used more than 2,200 animals in tests including 784 dogs, 424 primates, and 424 rabbits</cite>. <cite index="49-8">Eli Lilly has tormented a total of 3,400 mice and rats in the forced swim test since 1993</cite>. <cite index="49-9">Following talks with PETA, numerous competitors including Johnson & Johnson, Bayer, GlaxoSmithKline, AbbVie, Roche, AstraZeneca, Novo Nordisk, Boehringer Ingelheim, Pfizer, and Bristol Myers Squibb have all dropped the forced swim test</cite>, but Eli Lilly has not.

guns.s: Not applicable. Eli Lilly is a pharmaceutical company and does not manufacture, sell, or distribute firearms.

privacy.s: <cite index="76-3">Eli Lilly violated its own policy on patient confidentiality by publicizing the email addresses of over 700 patients taking the drug Prozac</cite> in 2001. <cite index="75-9">Under the FTC consent order, Lilly must implement a four-stage security program that includes better training and supervision of employees, internal and external risk assessments, and annual review of the security program</cite>. Company has since adopted data privacy frameworks including <cite index="72-19">EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework Principles with regard to personal information received from the European Union</cite>.

execPay.s: <cite index="67-1">In 2024, CEO David Ricks' compensation grew 10% to $29.2 million</cite>. <cite index="68-1">This represents an 87% jump since he assumed the role in 2017</cite>. <cite index="68-11">Of Ricks' $29.2 million total pay package, $19.7 million (67%) was in equity awards</cite>. Specific CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio not disclosed in available sources.
